name: Google Docs
overview:
Google Docs is a real-time collaborative document editor where multiple users can simultaneously edit the
same document and see each other's changes instantly. The core challenge is conflict resolution: when two
users edit the same part of a document simultaneously, their changes must be merged correctly without data
loss. The system must handle millions of concurrent documents, support rich text formatting, maintain a
complete edit history, and work reliably over unreliable network connections.

inScope: Persistent document storage with full edit history
Cursor presence (see where other users are editing)
Offline editing with sync on reconnect

items: Support millions of concurrent documents with multiple simultaneous editors per document
Operation propagation latency <100ms between collaborators on the same document
All clients must converge to the same document state — no data loss from concurrent edits
Offline edits must sync correctly on reconnect, even after hundreds of concurrent remote operations
Full edit history retained indefinitely; snapshot every 1000 ops for fast recovery

entities.name: Document
entities.schema:
{
  doc_id: UUID,
  owner_id: string,
  title: string,

  content: string,  // current state
  version: int,
  created_at: timestamp,
  updated_at: timestamp
}

entities.name: Operation
entities.schema:
{
  op_id: UUID,
  doc_id: UUID,
  user_id: string,
  type: enum(INSERT|DELETE|RETAIN),
  position: int,
  content: string,  // for INSERT
  length: int,      // for DELETE/RETAIN
  version: int,     // document version this op was based on
  timestamp: ISO8601
}

endpoints.response: { operations: [{ op_id, user_id, type, position, content, timestamp }] }

approaches.title: Last Write Wins
approaches.description: The last operation received by the server overwrites any conflicting operations. Data loss is unacceptable for a document editor. If two users type simultaneously, one user's changes are silently discarded.
approaches.tradeoff: Data loss; unacceptable for collaborative editing.

approaches.title: Operational Transformation (OT)
approaches.description: Each operation is transformed against concurrent operations before being applied. The transform function adjusts positions to account for insertions/deletions by other users. OT is the algorithm used by Google Docs. It guarantees convergence (all clients reach the same state) and intention preservation (your edit does what you intended). OT is complex to implement correctly, especially for rich text with formatting operations.
approaches.tradeoff: Complex to implement; requires a central server to serialize operations; hard to extend to new

approaches.title: operation types.
approaches.description: CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) [RECOMMENDED] Use a CRDT (specifically a sequence CRDT like RGA or LSEQ) to represent the document. Each character has a unique ID; insertions and deletions are commutative and idempotent. CRDTs don't require a central server for conflict resolution — clients can merge changes peer-to-peer. Offline editing is naturally supported. For this design, we use OT (as Google Docs does) for its proven track record, but note CRDTs as the modern alternative.
approaches.tradeoff: CRDTs have higher memory overhead per character; some CRDT implementations have

approaches.title: tombstone accumulation issues.
approaches.description: Operation Ordering Client Timestamps Use client-side timestamps to order operations. Clock skew between clients makes this unreliable. Two operations with the same timestamp have undefined ordering.
approaches.tradeoff: Clock skew causes inconsistent ordering across clients.

approaches.title: Server-Side Sequencing
approaches.description: The server assigns a monotonically increasing sequence number to each operation. All clients apply operations in sequence number order. The server is the single source of truth for operation ordering. Clients buffer out-of-order operations and apply them in sequence. The server uses a single-writer model per document (one server owns each document) to ensure sequential numbering without coordination.
approaches.tradeoff: Single server per document limits throughput; server is a bottleneck for very popular

components.name: Collaboration Server
components.responsibility: Owns a set of documents; receives operations via WebSocket; applies

components.name: Operation Log (Kafka)
components.responsibility: Durable log of all operations per document; enables replay and history

components.name: Document Store
components.responsibility: (Spanner) Stores current document state and version; globally consistent

components.name: Operation History
components.responsibility: (Bigtable) Stores all historical operations; row key = doc_id + sequence_number

components.name: Presence Service
components.responsibility: Tracks cursor positions and active collaborators; uses Redis pub/sub

components.name: Load Balancer (sticky)
components.responsibility: Routes all connections for a given doc_id to the same Collaboration

components.name: Snapshot Service
components.responsibility: Periodically snapshots document state to reduce replay time on server restart

questions.title: 2. Offline Editing
questions.content:
When a client goes offline, it continues editing locally. Operations are buffered in the client's local storage. On
reconnect, the client sends all buffered operations to the server. The server transforms each buffered operation
against all operations that occurred while the client was offline (using the client's last known version as the
base). This can involve transforming against hundreds of operations for long offline periods.

questions.title: 3. Document Snapshots
questions.content:
Replaying all operations from the beginning to reconstruct a document is too slow for large documents. The
Snapshot Service takes a snapshot of the document state every 1000 operations. On server restart, the
document is loaded from the latest snapshot and only the operations since the snapshot are replayed.
Snapshots are stored in Bigtable alongside the operation log.

questions.title: 4. Cursor Presence
questions.content:
Each client sends cursor position updates every 500ms (or on cursor movement). The Presence Service stores
cursor positions in Redis (key = doc_id:user_id, value = position, TTL = 5 seconds). When a cursor update is
received, it's broadcast to all other clients editing the same document via Redis pub/sub. Cursor positions must
be transformed against incoming operations (same OT logic) to remain accurate.

questions.title: 5. Access Control
questions.content:
Documents have three permission levels: OWNER, EDITOR, VIEWER. The Collaboration Server checks
permissions on WebSocket connection. VIEWERs receive operation broadcasts but their own operations are
rejected. Sharing is managed via a separate Sharing Service that updates the document's ACL in Spanner.
Permission changes take effect within 30 seconds (ACL cache TTL).

broadcast cursor
snapshot trigger
keyFlows: ① Client sends op → Load Balancer (sticky) → Collab Server
② Collab Server applies OT transform → assigns sequence number
③ Transformed op broadcast to all other clients via WebSocket
④ Op published to Kafka → persisted to Bigtable (history) + Spanner (state)
⑤ Snapshot Svc takes state snapshot every 1000 ops for fast recovery

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Operational Transformation in depth: read the original Jupiter OT paper, understand
the transformation functions for all operation type combinations, and why OT requires a central server.
Expert Note: Learn about CRDTs for text editing: the RGA (Replicated Growable Array) and LSEQ
algorithms, how they assign unique IDs to characters, and how they handle concurrent insertions at the
same position.
Expert Note: Understand Google Spanner's TrueTime API: how it uses GPS and atomic clocks to provide
globally consistent timestamps, enabling external consistency without distributed locks.
Expert Note: Study the Diamond Problem in OT: why naive OT implementations fail for 3+ concurrent
users, and how the Jupiter protocol solves it with a client-server architecture.
Expert Note: Learn about WebSocket connection management at scale: how to use consistent hashing to
route document connections to the same server, and how to handle server failures with connection
migration.
